---
title: "From Heterogeneous Biomedical Data to Patient Digital Twins. "
subtitle: "The Role of Knowledge Graphs in Cyber-Physical and Cyber-Biological Systems for E-Health"
author: "Pasquale De Meo"
institute: "University of Messina"
date: "July 17, 2026"
pdf-engine: xelatex
format:
  beamer:
    theme: "Madrid"
    colortheme: "default"
    fonttheme: "default"
    slidelevel: 2
    aspectratio: 169
    fontsize: 10pt
    incremental: false
    navigation: horizontal
    header-includes:
      - \usepackage{tikz}
      - \usetikzlibrary{positioning,arrows.meta,shapes.geometric,calc,fit}
      - \usepackage{pgfplots}
      - \usepackage{booktabs}
      - \usepackage{graphicx}
      - \usepackage{hyperref}
    include-in-header:
      text: |
        \setbeamertemplate{navigation symbols}{}
        \setbeamertemplate{footline}{
          \leavevmode\hbox{
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[wd=.33\paperwidth,ht=2.5ex,dp=1ex,center]{author in head/foot}
              \usebeamerfont{author in head/foot}\insertshortauthor
            \end{beamercolorbox}
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[wd=.34\paperwidth,ht=2.5ex,dp=1ex,center]{title in head/foot}
              \usebeamerfont{title in head/foot}\insertshorttitle
            \end{beamercolorbox}
            \begin{beamercolorbox}[wd=.33\paperwidth,ht=2.5ex,dp=1ex,right]{date in head/foot}
              \usebeamerfont{date in head/foot}\insertshortdate{}\hspace*{2em}
              \insertframenumber{} / \inserttotalframenumber\hspace*{2ex}
            \end{beamercolorbox}
          }
          \vskip0pt
        }
execute:
  echo: false
---


# The Semantic Bridge Thesis {.center}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=3pt, font=\footnotesize, text width=3cm, minimum height=9mm}
]
\node[box] (cps) at (0,0)   {CPS / IoT\\sensors \& devices};
\node[box] (kg)  at (5,0)   {Knowledge Graph\\FHIR $\cdot$ SNOMED CT $\cdot$ SOSA};
\node[box] (pdt) at (10,0)  {Patient\\Digital Twin};
\draw[->,very thick,double,double distance=1.2pt] (cps) -- node[fill=white,inner sep=1pt,font=\scriptsize]{semantic bridge} (kg);
\draw[->,very thick,double,double distance=1.2pt] (kg) -- node[fill=white,inner sep=1pt,font=\scriptsize]{reasoning} (pdt);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- Heterogeneous CPS/IoT streams are **syntactically rich but semantically poor**; KGs supply the missing meaning layer.
- A KG makes observations **queryable, reason-able, and clinically comparable** — the substrate on which a Twin is simulated.
- **Lecture thesis:** the *quality of the bridge* (ontologies + pipelines + reasoning), not the sensors, determines clinical trustworthiness.

---

# Roadmap {.center}

| Section | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Foundations & Standards** | Ontology stack, architectures, interop limits  |
| **2. CPS-to-KG Integration Pipelines** | Sensor→triple architectures, real-time vs batch  |
| **3. Digital Twin & AI Reasoning** | Rule-based / neuro-symbolic / RAG, validation  |
| **4. Open Challenges & Frontiers** | Scalability, privacy, benchmarks |

We focus on **eight** recent papers (2023–2026), but feel free to suggest any other relevant reference.




# 1 · Foundations & Standards
##  *"What language do the sensors speak?"*

- Goal: establish the **ontology vocabulary** and the **conceptual architecture** before any pipeline.
- Anchored by:
  - **Hendawi & Li (2024)** — clinical terminologies;
  - **Chatterjee et al. (2024)** — sensor ontologies;
  - **Carbonaro et al. (2023)** — blueprint.


---

## Healthcare Ontology Foundations: From Codes to Graphs

- **Core contribution:** A *Comprehensive Personal Health KG (PHKG)* unifying EHR, wearables, insurance, and **social determinants of health (SDoH)** under one OWL schema with **SNOMED CT + ICD-10** mapped to nodes.
- Privacy is **schema-native** (role-based access control embedded in the KG), not bolted on.
- SPARQL exposes **longitudinal/temporal** queries.
- **Ontology used:** OWL · SNOMED CT (clinical) · ICD-10 (administrative).


**Reference.** Hendawi, R., & Li, J. (2024). *Comprehensive personal health knowledge graph for effective management and utilization of personal health data*. In **Proceedings of IEEE AIMHC** (pp. 1–8). IEEE. https://doi.org/10.1109/aimhc59811.2024.00026

---

## Sensor Semantics: The SSN/SOSA Bridge

- **Core contribution:** Annotates **MOX2-5 accelerometer** observations as OWL/RDF triples via W3C **SSN/SOSA**
- Converts **CSV → RDF** and compares real vs. synthetic activity data through SPARQL.
- Formally models **sensor · observation · feature-of-interest · result**, yielding device-agnostic, interoperable activity graphs with an explicit **FHIR integration pathway**.
- **Ontology used:** W3C SSN/SOSA (observation structure), candidate FHIR alignment.

**Reference.** Chatterjee, A., Gerdes, M., Prinz, A., Riegler, M., & Martínez, S. (2024). *Semantic representation and comparative analysis of physical activity sensor observations using MOX2-5 sensor in real and synthetic datasets: A proof-of-concept study*. **Scientific Reports**, *14*, Article 55183. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-55183-6

---

## Conceptual Blueprint: CONNECTED (PKG + DT)

- **Core contribution:** A systematic review distilled into a **layered architecture** — CPS/IoT acquisition → OWL/FHIR semantic alignment → **Personal Knowledge Graph** (evolving) → **DT simulation**.
- Formalises four requirements rarely co-located: 
  - **semantic interoperability**, 
  - **privacy-by-design**, 
  - **real-time data fusion**, 
  - **temporal reasoning**.
- **Ontology used:** OWL · FHIR (alignment layer); DT engine sits *above* the PKG.


---

## Ontology Stacking: One Patient, Many Vocabularies {.smaller}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=3pt, font=\footnotesize, text width=3cm, minimum height=7mm}
]
\node[box] (obs)    at (0,1.8)  {SSN / SOSA\\observation structure};
\node[box] (fhir)   at (0,0.6)  {HL7 FHIR\\exchange \& API};
\node[box] (snomed) at (0,-0.6) {SNOMED CT\\clinical terms};
\node[box] (icd)    at (0,-1.8) {ICD-10\\classification / billing};
\node[box] (owl)    at (5,0)    {OWL 2.0\\logic \& constraints};
\node[box] (phkg)   at (9.2,0)  {Personal Health\\Knowledge Graph};
\draw[->,semithick] (obs) -- (owl);
\draw[->,semithick] (fhir) -- (owl);
\draw[->,semithick] (snomed) -- (owl);
\draw[->,semithick] (icd) -- (owl);
\draw[->,semithick] (owl) -- (phkg);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- **FHIR** = exchange spine · **SNOMED CT** = clinical semantics · **ICD-10** = administrative granularity · **SSN/SOSA** = observation provenance · **OWL** = the logic that binds them.
- No single standard is canonical; the **KG is where they are reconciled** — which makes alignment the failure-prone bottleneck.




# 2 · CPS-to-KG Integration Pipelines
## *"From a voltage on a wrist to a triple in a graph"*

- The **bridge** is itself an engineered artifact: ingestion → mapping → triple materialisation → query.
- Four architectures, one spectrum: **how much happens at the edge vs. centrally, and how often**.


**Reference.** Pourvahab, M., Monteiro, A., Pais, S., & Pombo, N. (2025). *A unified semantic framework for IoT-healthcare data interoperability: A graph-based machine learning approach using RDF and R2RML*. In **Proceedings of the ACM Symposium on Applied Computing** (Article 3757054). ACM. https://doi.org/10.1145/3756681.3757054

---

## The Generic Sensor-to-Triple Pipeline

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=2pt, font=\scriptsize, text width=1.8cm, minimum height=9mm}
]
\node[box] (a) at (0,0)    {Physical CPS/IoT\\wearables $\cdot$ CGM $\cdot$ EEG};
\node[box] (b) at (2.1,0)  {Raw data\\CSV / JSON / streams};
\node[box] (c) at (4.2,0)  {Ontology mapping\\R2RML $\cdot$ SOSA $\cdot$ FHIR};
\node[box] (d) at (6.3,0)  {RDF Knowledge Graph\\OWL triples};
\node[box] (e) at (8.4,0)  {Reasoning \& query\\SPARQL $\cdot$ SWRL $\cdot$ ML};
\node[box] (f) at (10.5,0) {Patient Digital Twin};
\draw[->,semithick] (a) -- (b);
\draw[->,semithick] (b) -- (c);
\draw[->,semithick] (c) -- (d);
\draw[->,semithick] (d) -- (e);
\draw[->,semithick] (e) -- (f);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- Every Section-2 paper is an **instantiation** of stages B→D; they differ in *mapping language, where materialisation occurs, and update frequency*.
- The honest engineering question is **not** "RDF or not?" but "real-time materialisation vs. batch re-build?"


---

## Unified Semantic Framework: RDF + R2RML Ingestion

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=3pt, font=\footnotesize, text width=2.6cm, minimum height=8mm}
]
\node[box] (w)    at (0,1.2)   {Wearables \& smart devices};
\node[box] (e)    at (0,-0.6)  {EHR / relational DBs};
\node[box] (r2)   at (3.4,0.3) {R2RML mapping rules\\RDB $\rightarrow$ RDF};
\node[box] (dist) at (6.4,2.1) {Distributed graph-build layer\\real-time ingestion};
\node[box] (kg)   at (6.4,0.3) {RDF Knowledge Graph\\OWL-aligned $\cdot$ FHIR vocab};
\node[box] (ml)   at (9.6,0.3) {Graph-based ML pipeline\\inference / extraction};
\node[box] (ci)   at (9.6,-1.6){Clinical intelligence};
\draw[->,semithick] (w) -- (r2);
\draw[->,semithick] (e) -- (r2);
\draw[->,semithick] (r2) -- (kg);
\draw[->,semithick,dashed] (dist) -- node[right,fill=white,inner sep=1pt,font=\scriptsize]{scales} (kg);
\draw[->,semithick] (kg) -- (ml);
\draw[->,semithick] (ml) -- (ci);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- **Architecture (Pourvahab et al., 2025):** R2RML declaratively maps relational healthcare data into OWL-aligned triples; a **distributed graph-construction layer** absorbs real-time IoT volume.
- **Transformation steps:** relational row → R2RML template → RDF triple → OWL-classified node → FHIR-compatible vocabulary.
- **Real-time vs. batch trade-off:** *ingestion* is real-time, but the **graph-ML inference** is batch over the constructed KG → low-latency alerts need a streaming bypass.



---

## Wearable CPS-to-KG Construction (SSN/SOSA)

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=2pt, font=\scriptsize, text width=2.3cm, minimum height=8mm}
]
\node[box] (s)    at (0,0.6)    {MOX2-5 accelerometer\\physical activity};
\node[box] (csv)  at (2.7,0.6)  {CSV sensor export};
\node[box] (ann)  at (5.4,0.6)  {SSN/SOSA annotation\\sensor / obs / feature / result};
\node[box] (rdf)  at (8.1,0.6)  {OWL-compliant RDF graph};
\node[box] (q)    at (10.8,0.6) {SPARQL query \& comparison};
\node[box] (fhir) at (8.1,-1.2) {FHIR-compliant clinical repo};
\draw[->,semithick] (s) -- (csv);
\draw[->,semithick] (csv) -- (ann);
\draw[->,semithick] (ann) -- (rdf);
\draw[->,semithick] (rdf) -- (q);
\draw[->,semithick] (rdf) -- (fhir);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- **Architecture (Chatterjee et al., 2024):** a **concrete CSV→RDF** pipeline — the most explicit "how to triplify a wearable" reference in the set.
- **Transformation steps:** raw accelerometry → activity classification → SOSA `sosa:Observation` with `hasResult` → linked to `FeatureOfInterest` (patient).
- **Real-time vs. batch trade-off:** proof-of-concept is **offline/batch** (file export); no native streaming → gains simplicity & reproducibility, loses latency, so unsuitable as-is for acute monitoring.


---

## IoT-KG-Digital-Twin: A Three-Layer Pipeline

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=3pt, font=\footnotesize, text width=3.2cm, minimum height=8mm}
]
\node[box] (l1)  at (0,2.2)  {Layer 1 --- Wearable IoT\\EEG $\cdot$ GSR $\cdot$ heart rate};
\node[box] (l2)  at (0,0.7)  {Layer 2 --- Semantic interop\\IoT-Lite + SOSA/SSN $\rightarrow$ RDF};
\node[box] (l3)  at (0,-0.8) {Layer 3 --- KG-driven Digital Twin\\mental-health trajectories};
\node[box] (int) at (5,-0.8) {Personalised interventions};
\node[box] (std) at (5,1.4)  {HL7 FHIR $\cdot$ SNOMED CT $\cdot$ W3C stack};
\draw[->,semithick] (l1) -- (l2);
\draw[->,semithick] (l2) -- (l3);
\draw[->,semithick] (l3) -- (int);
\draw[->,semithick,dashed] (std) -- node[fill=white,inner sep=1pt,font=\scriptsize]{cross-domain reasoning} (l3);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- **Architecture (Gyrard et al., 2024):** sensor layer → semantic-annotation layer (IoT-Lite + SOSA/SSN) → KG-driven DT that models **patient mental-health trajectories**.
- **Transformation steps:** physiological signal → SOSA observation → KG node typed by SNOMED CT → **temporal SPARQL** detects burnout/depression patterns.
- **Real-time vs. batch trade-off:** continuous *emotion monitoring* is near-real-time, but **reasoning is query-driven (semi-batch)** → good for screening, insufficient for crisis intervention.



---

## FHIR-Native Ingestion & Model Binding

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=2pt, font=\scriptsize, text width=1.9cm, minimum height=9mm}
]
\node[box] (dt)   at (0,0)    {EHR + IoT time-stamped streams};
\node[box] (api)  at (2.2,0)  {FHIR R4 REST API};
\node[box] (kg)   at (4.4,0)  {Persistent OWL-DL KG\\semantic backbone};
\node[box] (mimo) at (6.6,0)  {MIMO ontology\\standardises model I/O};
\node[box] (bind) at (8.8,0)  {Manifest-based binding\\auto model registration};
\node[box] (pdt)  at (11.0,0) {Multi-AI Patient Digital Twin\\e.g., stroke risk};
\draw[->,semithick] (dt) -- (api);
\draw[->,semithick] (api) -- (kg);
\draw[->,semithick] (kg) -- (mimo);
\draw[->,semithick] (mimo) -- (bind);
\draw[->,semithick] (bind) -- (pdt);
\draw[->,semithick] (kg) to[bend left=35] node[above,fill=white,inner sep=1pt,font=\tiny]{automatic gap detection} (pdt);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- **Architecture (Marfoglia et al., 2026):** a **FHIR R4 REST API** retrieves time-stamped EHR/IoT streams into a persistent **OWL-DL KG**; the novel **MIMO ontology** standardises every AI model's inputs/outputs for transparent, reusable composition.
- **Transformation steps:** FHIR resource → OWL-DL assertion → MIMO interface match → manifest-driven binding → multi-AI pipeline.
- **Real-time vs. batch trade-off:** real-time API *retrieval* + automatic **gap detection** when sensor coverage is insufficient, but **OWL-DL inference latency** under load is the throttle.


---

## Real-Time vs. Batch: Cross-Architecture Trade-offs {.smaller}

| Architecture | Ingestion | Materialisation | Reasoning cadence | Best fit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Pourvahab 2025** (RDF/R2RML) | real-time (distributed) | on-write | batch ML | large-scale analytics |
| **Chatterjee 2024** (CSV→SOSA) | offline/batch | on-load | query-time | research / reproducibility |
| **Gyrard 2024** (3-layer) | near-real-time | on-annotation | semi-batch SPARQL | screening / prevention |
| **Marfoglia 2026** (FHIR+MIMO) | real-time API | on-retrieval | on-demand OWL-DL | clinical decision support |

- **Universal pattern:** ingestion moves toward real-time; *reasoning* stays batch/periodic — the gap between them is the clinical-latency bottleneck.
- None of the four provides **streaming incremental reasoning** with bounded guarantees.



# 3 · Digital Twin & AI Reasoning
## *"A graph that thinks — but how, and is it right?"*

- Reasoning families in play: **rule-based (OWL-DL / SWRL)**, **neuro-symbolic / RAG**, and **query-driven** temporal inference.
- For each: mechanism → validation methodology → **clinical applicability gap**.


---

## The Reasoning Mechanism Landscape {.smaller}

```{=latex}
\begin{center}
\begin{tikzpicture}[
  >=Stealth,
  box/.style={draw, rounded corners, align=center, fill=blue!6, inner sep=3pt, font=\footnotesize, text width=3cm, minimum height=8mm}
]
\node[box] (r)   at (0,1.2)   {Rule-based\\OWL-DL $\cdot$ SWRL};
\node[box] (n)   at (0,0)     {Neuro-symbolic\\KG embeddings + rules};
\node[box] (q)   at (0,-1.2)  {RAG / query-driven\\LLM over SPARQL/KG};
\node[box] (t)   at (4.7,0)   {Patient Digital Twin};
\node[box] (dss) at (8.7,0)   {Clinical decision support};
\draw[->,semithick] (r) -- (t);
\draw[->,semithick] (n) -- (t);
\draw[->,semithick] (q) -- (t);
\draw[->,semithick] (t) -- (dss);
\end{tikzpicture}
\end{center}
```

- **Rule-based (OWL-DL/SWRL):** transparent, auditable, but brittle & authoring-heavy — used by Marfoglia (2026), Krämer (2025), Sarani Rad (2024).
- **Neuro-symbolic / RAG:** emerging for clinical QA; trades some auditability for adaptivity — *not yet grounded* in this corpus's deployed systems.
- **Common gap across all:** scarce prospective clinical validation.


---

## OWL-DL + MIMO Reasoning for Patient Digital Twins

- **Reasoning mechanism (Marfoglia et al., 2026):** **OWL-DL inference** enforces semantic consistency and **auto-triggers gap detection** when sensor coverage is insufficient; the **MIMO ontology** standardises AI model I/O for dynamic, manifest-driven multi-model composition.
- **Validation:** benchmarked end-to-end on **stroke-risk prediction** with real-world, time-aware clinical streams → demonstrates KG-to-PDT scalability.
- **Clinical applicability gap:** no prospective trial; generalisation beyond stroke untested; **OWL-DL reasoning latency under concurrent load** unquantified.

---

## Federated SWRL Reasoning: Privacy-First Patient Twins

- **Reasoning mechanism (Krämer, 2025):** the **Patient Medical Digital Twin (PMDT)** in **OWL 2.0** with five clinical Blueprints; **SWRL rules executed at the edge** over multimodal IoT time-series; only **OWL class assertions** (never raw data) are propagated centrally.
- **Validation:** synthetic patient scenarios in **diabetes & heart failure** — checks *completeness of ontological coverage* and *soundness of temporal inference chains*.
- **Clinical applicability gap:** synthetic-only, no real deployment; SWRL coverage depends on **expert rule authoring**; federated reasoning untested at scale.

---

## PHKG-Driven Diabetes Twin: Reasoning on a Live Graph

- **Reasoning mechanism (Sarani Rad et al., 2024):** a **Personal Health KG (PHKG)** is the digital twin's substrate — it **auto-updates** as CGM/EHR/PROM observations arrive; **OWL reasoning infers hypoglycaemia-risk patterns** and medication-adjustment cues (HL7 FHIR for exchange).
- **Validation:** evaluated on a **real patient cohort**; superior glycaemic-outcome prediction vs. rule-based baselines.
- **Clinical applicability gap:** single disease, **retrospective cohort**, no prospective RCT; **PHKG update latency** under high-frequency CGM unaddressed.


---

## Reasoning Cross-Check: Mental-Health Twin

- **Reasoning mechanism (Gyrard et al., 2024):** **temporal SPARQL** queries against the patient KG detect burnout/depression trajectories; SOSA/SSN + SNOMED CT + FHIR enable cross-domain clinical joins.
- **Validation:** proof-of-concept **use-case demonstration** linked to SDG-3 targets — *no clinical-cohort benchmark*.
- **Clinical applicability gap:** affect lacks clean SNOMED CT representation → reasoning rests on **under-specified clinical semantics**; intervention triggering unvalidated.



# 4 · Open Challenges & Research Frontiers
## *"Where the graph still breaks"*

- Synthesise across **all eight papers** on four axes: **scalability, temporal reasoning, privacy/federation, evaluation benchmarks**.
- Goal: convert gaps into **three PhD-defining research questions**.


---

## Cross-Paper Synthesis Matrix {.smaller}

| Paper (year) | Scalability | Temporal reasoning | Privacy / federated | Evaluation |
|---|:--:|:--:|:--:|:--:|
| Pourvahab et al. (2025) | Yes: distributed layer | — | No | Yes: interop metrics |
| Gyrard et al. (2024) | Part | Yes: temporal SPARQL | No | Part: use-case demo |
| Marfoglia et al. (2026) | Yes: end-to-end bench | Yes: time-aware | No | Yes: stroke benchmark |
| Carbonaro et al. (2023) | — | Yes: identified req | Yes: privacy-by-design | — conceptual |
| Krämer (2025) | Part | Yes: temporal inference | Yes: class-assertion fed. | Part: synthetic only |
| Sarani Rad et al. (2024) | Part | Yes: incremental PHKG | No | Yes: real cohort |
| Chatterjee et al. (2024) | — | — | No | Yes: real vs synthetic |
| Hendawi & Li (2024) | Part: prototype | Yes: longitudinal | Yes: RBAC schema | Yes: real EHR |

- Legend: **Yes** = addressed · **Part** = partial · **No / —** = absent. Privacy is the rarest axis; benchmarks are inconsistent.

---

## Scalability × Temporal Reasoning

- **The contradiction:** CPS streams demand *fresh* graphs; OWL-DL/SWRL reasoning is **non-incremental** → re-materialisation cost grows with graph size.
- Only **Pourvahab (2025)** and **Marfoglia (2026)** tackle scale, and neither provides **streaming incremental reasoning with bounded latency**.
- Temporal support is mostly **query-time** (SPARQL) or **scenario-based** (Krämer), not a first-class temporal model (e.g., valid-time RDF).


---

## Privacy-Preserving & Federated KGs

- **Best in class:** Krämer (2025) propagates *only OWL class assertions*; Hendawi & Li (2024) embed **RBAC in the schema**; Carbonaro (2023) elevates **privacy-by-design** to a requirement.
- **Collective gap:** no **differential privacy, secure multi-party computation, or verifiable federation** over health KGs; **inference-based re-identification** of rare patients is unanalysed.
- Consent + governance (CONNECTED's open agenda) remain **unimplemented** in any reviewed system.


---

## Evaluation Benchmarks: The Missing Yardstick

- Each paper invents **its own** dataset/metric — interop metrics (Pourvahab), SPARQL coverage (Chatterjee), cohort AUC (Sarani Rad), scalability bench (Marfoglia), synthetic completeness (Krämer).
- There is **no shared CPS-KG-PDT benchmark** measuring semantic interoperability *and* inference latency *and* twin fidelity together.
- Synthetic-only validation (Krämer, Gyrard) cannot certify clinical soundness.


---

## Three Open PhD Research Questions {.smaller}

::: {.callout-tip title="RQ1 — Streaming Temporal Reasoning"}
How can we perform **incremental temporal reasoning** (valid-time RDF + delta-aware OWL/SWRL) over CPS/IoT streams with **bounded, clinically-tolerable latency** — instead of periodic batch re-materialisation?
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="RQ2 — Verifiably-Private Federated KG Reasoning"}
Can we design **federated neuro-symbolic reasoning** that never exposes raw patient triples *or* re-identifying graph structure, with **provable privacy guarantees and full auditability** (GDPR Art. 22)?
:::

::: {.callout-tip title="RQ3 — A Shared CPS-KG-PDT Benchmark"}
What benchmark suite (datasets, interop/latency/fidelity metrics, baselines) would let heterogeneous KG-PDT architectures be compared on equal terms — and what synthetic→real data ladder makes it clinically credible?
:::


---

## Key Takeaways {.center}

- **KGs are the bridge** — but the bridge's quality is set by *ontology alignment*, not sensor count.
- **Ingestion is real-time; reasoning is not** — that gap *is* the clinical-latency problem.
- **Rule-based reasoning dominates**; neuro-symbolic/RAG remain promising but ungrounded clinically.
- **Privacy and benchmarks are the twin blind spots** of the entire corpus.
- A defensible PhD lives where the matrix has **a "No" on a deployment-critical axis**.


---

## References {.smaller}


- Carbonaro, A., Marfoglia, A., Nardini, F., & Mellone, S. (2023). CONNECTED: Leveraging digital twins and personal knowledge graphs in healthcare digitalization. *Frontiers in Digital Health*, *5*, Article 1322428. https://doi.org/10.3389/fdgth.2023.1322428
- Chatterjee, A., Gerdes, M., Prinz, A., Riegler, M., & Martínez, S. (2024). Semantic representation and comparative analysis of physical activity sensor observations using MOX2-5 sensor in real and synthetic datasets: A proof-of-concept study. *Scientific Reports*, *14*, Article 55183. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-024-55183-6
- Gyrard, A., Mohammadi, S., Gaur, M., & Kung, A. (2024). *IoT-based preventive mental health using knowledge graphs and standards for better well-being* [Preprint]. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2406.13791
- Hendawi, R., & Li, J. (2024). Comprehensive personal health knowledge graph for effective management and utilization of personal health data. In *Proceedings of the IEEE 2nd International Conference on AI in Medicine and Health Care (AIMHC)* (pp. 1–8). IEEE. https://doi.org/10.1109/aimhc59811.2024.00026
- Krämer, B. J. (2025). *A semantic framework for patient digital twins in chronic care* [Preprint]. arXiv. https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2510.09134
- Marfoglia, A., D'Errico, C., Mellone, S., & Carbonaro, A. (2026). A knowledge graph-driven framework for deploying AI-powered patient digital twins. *Future Generation Computer Systems*, Article 108380. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.future.2026.108380
- Pourvahab, M., Monteiro, A., Pais, S., & Pombo, N. (2025). A unified semantic framework for IoT-healthcare data interoperability: A graph-based machine learning approach using RDF and R2RML. In *Proceedings of the ACM Symposium on Applied Computing* (Article 3757054). ACM. https://doi.org/10.1145/3756681.3757054
- Sarani Rad, F., Hendawi, R., Yang, X., & Li, J. (2024). Personalized diabetes management with digital twins: A patient-centric knowledge graph approach. *Journal of Personalized Medicine*, *14*(4), Article 359. https://doi.org/10.3390/jpm14040359
